Are my personal standards congruent with my vision for the future? In other words, there are these two competing aspects of success. One is your vision for the future. You have to have a vision for the future that's bold and bright and clear and that it makes you jump out of bed in the morning to go and seize that vision, to go and achieve it, right? And it also has to have an integrated why, meaning why does the vision matter to you? And remember, it can't just be about money. Your vision has to be a visceral animal almost. It just sort of just envelops you and just pushes you out of bed each morning and just let you go through those barriers and obstacles and challenges that always pop up along the way and keep focused on that vision. And I always say that people who handle and drill and motivate themselves, they lack a powerful why. They have an integrated that why into their vision for the future. So make sure you have that vision that's bold, bright and clear, that makes you jump out of bed and it has to have a fully integrated why. That's one aspect, right? But now it's one to the other aspect, which is your personal standards, meaning what level of wealth or success must you have? What will you not settle for less than? What level that if you were below it would just literally make you fricking miserable? Like you just couldn't go on. You'd be obsessed with making more money. You'd be obsessed with taking action. You'd do anything. Make everything else in your life subordinate to making X amount of money, having X amount of success. What is that level? Now here's the deal. I've already told you that this is crucial in the past. Other trainings I've done, how crucial is to identify what your personal standards are if necessary, raise those standards. But the question is today is, well, how do I know whether I should raise them or not? All my standards high enough to ensure my success. And here's the answer. What comes down to does it support your vision? In other words, if you have a bold, bright, and incredibly robust vision for the future, and your standards are low, that's like a lack of congruency. There's dissonance there. Essentially you have a champagne vision and beer standards. Not going to work. So when you'll end up being is one of these hapless dreamers who goes and imagines my life being so great, but you never take action. That's what the result of that is. Flip side of the equation is if you have not a very bold, bright vision for the future, but you have this really high standard to make money. You want to make a lot of money, but you don't really have this vision of how you're going to do it and what it's going to look like. So what you find is that you're almost locked in the daily grind every single day. You're not motivated. You're one of these achiever types. All you do is work, work, work. You're chasing your tail like a f***ing gerbil, running around on a treadmill or a mountain or a house chasing its tail, so to speak, right? Or a dog chasing its tail, whatever the metaphor is, many of them, right? The point is that in that case you have basically a beer vision and champagne standards. That leads also to dissonance. The key is, and it starts with what's your vision for the future? You have to start there and work backwards and do a standards check. Make sure that you're standard, that what you must have and be honest with yourself. Listen, I know I have this vision for the future, but is that really me? Is that really what I demand of myself? Can I live without that? Let me tell you something. In your mind, you're saying, you know, I love to have this, but it doesn't really matter. I mean, at the end of the day, it doesn't have to. Not a big deal. That's not empowering. That's not going to make you jump out of bed each morning. That's not going to give you the emotional wear with all the backbone to jump out of bed and overcome any bad or obstacle. It ends up standing in your way or getting thrown at you because they always get thrown at you as you're going through the brass ring. Without those standards being congruent with the vision, you're not going to end up achieving it. Again, standard has to match vision. Starting with vision and then you do a standards check. Ask yourself that honest question again. This is with the other trainings I've done, so we're not going to go through the process of how you identify your own standard. We've already been through that. You all own the straight line system by now. I think everyone does. It's part of this, I believe. Those of you who don't, you should own it. It's unbelievable training. In fact, for those who don't, I'm working on a major discount. I think you all own it. Anyway, here's the deal. If your standard turns out to be too low, and it doesn't support your vision, it's not congruent, then you have to link pain to your current standard and then create a new standard and link pleasure to it, meaning identify the three things that are the worst outcomes that you couldn't live in your life. Link that to your current standard and then say, what are the three most important values for me? Things that make me just so happy. Link positive values to your higher standards.
